segments.text: Hello viewers, Tim Poole here on The Huddle and I'm with Gronja Hurst, CEO of the Betting
and Gaming Council.
Gronja, thanks for joining me.
Pleasure, Tim.
Pleased to be here.
So we have plenty to discuss, unless people have been living under a rock for the last
few months in the UK market, they'll understand that we've got some big topics ahead.
To start with though, most recently Michael Duggar announced, there was an announcement
that he's moving on as chairman.
Could you maybe talk us through a bit of maybe what the process is going to be there, but
also reflect on, of course, a big contribution from him to the BGC?
Yeah, so I've been really lucky to have Michael as my chair and mentor for the last 18 months.
Obviously as I transitioned into the CEO role, but also worked with him closely when I was
a member of the BGC.
I think he's effectively the founding father, if you like, of the BGC because he set it
up.
He gave it the structure, the purpose, the integrity, you know, he drove the championing
of industry standards and will therefore leave a really positive lasting legacy for the BGC.
He's got a few big boots to fill.
We're currently going through the process of looking at what we might need in that role
going forward, and then hopefully we'll be able to start recruiting soon.
But I'm sure he'll continue being a friend and an advocate for us as an industry, as
he always has been.
Yeah.
And I imagine you personally will probably be quite involved in that process because
you'll be working very closely with the new chairman.
Yeah, exactly.
I think obviously with the tax increases, it's a chance to kind of pause and reflect
what does the next kind of year, two, three years look like for the industry and therefore
what do we need in that position to help us achieve that?
I'm glad you mentioned the tax increases.
You know, I've been here for over seven years and every year there's been some discussion,
some threat of taxes might go up this year.
And it didn't really happen, especially to this degree, until this day.
I guess with a few months or a couple of months, how do you reflect on the fact that we are
now living in this new reality in the UK?
Yeah, it's still incredibly disappointing for everybody that works in the industry.
Obviously, 109,000 people employed in and around the sector, you know, obviously high
street jobs, but also high skilled jobs in tech outside of London and around the country.
So really hugely disappointing result for them from the government.
It's going to be deeply damaging, I would say, for the regulated sector, because as we
made the arguments throughout the campaign that the only real winner out of this whole
process is going to be the black market, when I never thought I'd say it, but the UK now
has one of the highest tax rates in the world.
You know, we've always been kind of seen as a proportionate, sensible place to do business
from a tax and regulatory position.
That is no longer the case now because of the political decision that the government
made as the regulated sector has to adapt and unfortunately shrink as a result of the
drastic tax increases, the black market is going to flourish.
That's bad for customers, it's bad for operators, it's bad for the Treasury, it's bad for sports.
I just simply don't understand the rationale behind the decision.
The policies and their impact on kind of, as you say, the UK was how it's been seen
in the past.
The impact on potentially other markets is something that's come up in this chair kind
of all week with people I've been talking to from America, from Poland, from, you know,
other European markets.
But when it comes to the process itself, there was a quote from Charles Cohen, a panel earlier
this week, and he said that in the lead up to this increase in tax, which is 40% RGD
from April, he said that we haven't just failed to lobby, we failed as an industry.
How do you respond to that?
Obviously, you guys are central in this kind of discussion, I guess.
So obviously, everybody is entitled to their own views.
I would completely disagree with that.
Obviously, the outcome of the budget was disastrous for the industry.
But that doesn't mean that the industry didn't run an effective campaign.
We led a data led, evidence driven, sustained campaign representing the industry, highlighting
all of the issues that would come to bear if the taxes were increased.
Unfortunately, the government took a political decision because of political reasons, which
is outside of the industry's control.
So I'd actually disagree that the industry failed in our efforts.
Obviously, we were hugely disappointed to see the outcome.
But I find it hard to see what more the industry collectively could have done to change the
government's mind.
So if you reflect on that, given what you've said about what you just said, that hard to
see what you could have done more, but the outcome that we had in the end, and as you
say, political and external factors, have you kind of had the chance to review strategically
and think, well, what can we do next?
What can we do differently, given that we've seen now how the government, the current government
is going to act in the face of the methods we used before?
Yeah, so I think there were some positives that came out of the budget, which we shouldn't
forget.
Obviously, machines gaming duty didn't increase.
Obviously, there was a positive outcome on bingo duty.
I think what could we have done differently?
We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously,
it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes.
And if they were, where would they fall?
You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and
maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views
on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit.
But again, as I say, at the end of the day, the government took a political decision.
And I do really feel like the industry put up the best possible fight they could, just
obviously didn't get the outcome we wanted.
Yeah.
I mean, it's good to kind of hear that immediate reflection of things that could have been
done differently.
And as you say, there's certainly a political factor, certainly in the UK, it's a vote winner
to bash gambling.
Unfortunately, that's just a reality that we kind of have to have to accept.
But the argument against black markets, which I think we all in the industry understand,
putting that forward, something I asked Stella David and Tain was, you know, we still see
And I will point out that Andrew Rhodes, I've interviewed him, and he said that compliance
has definitely improved over the years considerably.
But we still do see occasional fines.
And we see kind of AML, or not so much AML, but maybe social kind of failings from leading
regulated operators.
Does that perhaps undermine the black market argument?
Because you might just get a consumer maybe on Twitter saying, well, they've just been
fined for the same thing that the industry saying black markets, black market operators
are doing.
So it's a fair point.
But I would probably say that the it actually undermines the importance of the regulated
sector versus the illegal market, if when the fines or the regulatory settlements occur,
you know, these things are reported, their action, their sanction, the changes are made
within the regulated sector.
We now have a really good process where we share learnings among the regulated operators
and suppliers of things that they have learned through the process.
And that obviously drives up standards even more in the illegal, harmful black market.
You get none of that.
They obviously pay no tax, but more worryingly, they offer no protection whatsoever.
Customers don't know whether they're even going to get their money back.
You know, they don't know where that money is going to, what it might be funding.
So I don't think it undermines it.
I think it probably underlines the importance of having robust regulations in the regulated
sector so that customers know what they're getting and who they're who they're gaming
with.
Fair enough.
In one of the select committee hearings before and in the lead up to, you know, the disastrous
budget for the industry, there was a quote you made about, in fact, I don't remember
the exact quote, but there was you'll know which one I'm referring to because there was
a almost shows you perhaps what the industry is up against.
There was a bit of a backlash, arguably even overreaction, you know, but to something you
said in a hearing, obviously, certain newspapers, you know, were quick to run headlines.
But I don't know if you maybe wanted to clarify what you what you either what you actually
said or just how you reflect on on the reaction that that kind of thing got.
Yeah.
So the point I was trying to make was one of proportionality, which I think it can be
tricky now, having been there to do that in that sort of setting.
The point I'm trying to make is that obviously gambling related harm can be hugely negative
for individuals and also their families.
And there's nobody that takes that more seriously than the regulated sector.
That's why we have a range of measures in place, whether that's deposit limits, time
out, self exclusion, you know, AI and technology to track behavioral players, to track behavioral
markers in players.
But actually, the evidence is clear that the vast, vast majority of people, 22 and a half
million every month in the UK, do gamble safely as part of their leisure and entertainment.
We know from the NHS statistics that 0.4 percent of the adult population have a problem
with their gambling harm.
And therefore, you cannot say that gambling causes social harm per se.
Of course, it does for a small minority, but it doesn't for the vast majority.
And I actually think it's quite ironic, sadly, that the individuals on the select committee
who seem so concerned about harm are actually the same individuals that have been proposing
policies which are now going to come into force that will actually increase problem
gambling related harm by pushing customers to the black market.
Strategically, how do you think or or, you know, I'm sure there's been discussions with
members without giving any any trade secrets away, but how do you think operators might
might adapt?
I mean, a speculative one for me.
But is it worth potentially focusing a little more on sports betting than online casino
simply because there's now a 15 percent higher margin on it or lower margin?
Yeah, so I don't think it's any secret that the businesses are having to go through a
root and branch strategic review because the taxes are increasing so much that they are
going to have to look at three real levers, you know, product, people and promotions.
There are going to be efficiencies across all of those areas, including high street
venues.
We're going to see offers and odds reduced.
We're going to see, you know, less places.
We're going to see reduced return to player.
And I think more broadly, the businesses are going to have to look at diversifying outside
of the UK because the UK, quite frankly, is now not an attractive market to be when you
compare it to the rest of the world.
So unfortunately, I think you will see less investment and innovation in the UK as a result
of the budget increases.
Will operators try and persuade more players into sports versus casino?
I don't think that's doable because there is one P&L, right?
So there's lots of benefits of people playing on both casino and sports.
I think if you were to put in a kind of weird incentive, that just doesn't work for the
player, doesn't work for the operator.
So I don't see that happening.
Looking ahead to 2026, I want to put a positive, you know, put a positive frame on a question
because obviously it's, I used this phrase earlier this week, that the reality of our
situation kind of dictates what we have to talk about.
But on a positive note, what can we expect from the BGC in terms of campaigns, events
and certainly the AGM, which is a big event every year, is coming up soon.
So obviously, probably no surprise that the big focus for us will be on the black market.
So in its entirety, I would say, so how do we track it?
How do we monitor it?
How do we know if it's growing, how much it's growing by, who's operating in it, but also
trying to get some real traction on trying to prevent it.
So whether that's with the payment providers or the internet service providers, or those
that are marketing and promoting the sites, really kind of putting some pressure on them
to stop doing what they're doing.
So that will be a key focus for us.
Also improving and continuing to adapt our safer gambling commitments, you know, funding
and supporting sports.
Those are probably the key focuses of our AGM.
Well, Grainne, thank you very much for your time.
It's been a busy, busy week here at ICE, and I'm sure you're going to have a busy year
ahead.
So thanks for joining us.
Pleasure, Tim.
Thanks.

I think obviously with the tax increases, it's a chance to kind of pause and reflect what does the next kind of year, two, three years look like for the industry and therefore what do we need in that position to help us achieve that? I'm glad you mentioned the tax increases. You know, I've been here for over seven years and every year there's been some discussion, some threat of taxes might go up this year. And it didn't really happen, especially to this degree, until this day.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: You know, we've always been kind of seen as a proportionate, sensible place to do business from a tax and regulatory position. That is no longer the case now because of the political decision that the government made as the regulated sector has to adapt and unfortunately shrink as a result of the drastic tax increases, the black market is going to flourish. That's bad for customers, it's bad for operators, it's bad for the Treasury, it's bad for sports. I just simply don't understand the rationale behind the decision. The policies and their impact on kind of, as you say, the UK was how it's been seen in the past.
mergedCandidates.title: UK Tax Hikes Fuel Black Market Boom
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: bold claim—UK now among highest taxed markets. Payoff: immediate multi-party harm (customers, operators, Treasury, sports) with high urgency.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: We're going to see offers and odds reduced. We're going to see, you know, less places. We're going to see reduced return to player. And I think more broadly, the businesses are going to have to look at diversifying outside of the UK because the UK, quite frankly, is now not an attractive market to be when you compare it to the rest of the world. So unfortunately, I think you will see less investment and innovation in the UK as a result of the budget increases. Will operators try and persuade more players into sports versus casino? I don't think that's doable because there is one P&L, right? So there's lots of benefits of people playing on both casino and sports.
mergedCandidates.title: Expect Reduced Odds And Fewer Venues
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: consumer-facing, concrete harms—odds and places will shrink. Payoff: predicts less investment and innovation in UK, actionable and vivid.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: Yeah, so I don't think it's any secret that the businesses are having to go through a root and branch strategic review because the taxes are increasing so much that they are going to have to look at three real levers, you know, product, people and promotions. There are going to be efficiencies across all of those areas, including high street venues. We're going to see offers and odds reduced. We're going to see, you know, less places. We're going to see reduced return to player. And I think more broadly, the businesses are going to have to look at diversifying outside of the UK because the UK, quite frankly, is now not an attractive market to be when you compare it to the rest of the world.
mergedCandidates.title: Taxes Are Shrinking The UK Market
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: concrete consequences—reduced offers, fewer venues, lower RTP. Payoff: predicts less UK investment and forced diversification abroad, high-stakes forecast.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: Unfortunately, that's just a reality that we kind of have to have to accept. But the argument against black markets, which I think we all in the industry understand, putting that forward, something I asked Stella David and Tain was, you know, we still see And I will point out that Andrew Rhodes, I've interviewed him, and he said that compliance has definitely improved over the years considerably. But we still do see occasional fines. And we see kind of AML, or not so much AML, but maybe social kind of failings from leading regulated operators. Does that perhaps undermine the black market argument? Because you might just get a consumer maybe on Twitter saying, well, they've just been fined for the same thing that the industry saying black markets, black market operators are doing.
mergedCandidates.title: Fines Don’t Debunk Regulated Safety
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: counters a common skeptic point—regulated fines exist. Payoff: explains why regulation still protects customers while black market offers none.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously, it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes. And if they were, where would they fall? You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit. But again, as I say, at the end of the day, the government took a political decision. And I do really feel like the industry put up the best possible fight they could, just obviously didn't get the outcome we wanted. Yeah. I mean, it's good to kind of hear that immediate reflection of things that could have been done differently. And as you say, there's certainly a political factor, certainly in the UK, it's a vote winner to bash gambling.
mergedCandidates.title: Gambling Is A UK Vote-Winner
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: provocative political insight—bashing gambling wins votes. Payoff: explains why policy choices ignore industry evidence and raise black market risk.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: Unfortunately, the government took a political decision because of political reasons, which is outside of the industry's control. So I'd actually disagree that the industry failed in our efforts. Obviously, we were hugely disappointed to see the outcome. But I find it hard to see what more the industry collectively could have done to change the government's mind. So if you reflect on that, given what you've said about what you just said, that hard to see what you could have done more, but the outcome that we had in the end, and as you say, political and external factors, have you kind of had the chance to review strategically and think, well, what can we do next? What can we do differently, given that we've seen now how the government, the current government is going to act in the face of the methods we used before? Yeah, so I think there were some positives that came out of the budget, which we shouldn't forget.
mergedCandidates.title: Industry Didn’t Fail—Politics Decided
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: rebuttal to claim of lobbying failure. Payoff: explains a data-led campaign still lost to political choice—clear cause and outcome.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit. But again, as I say, at the end of the day, the government took a political decision. And I do really feel like the industry put up the best possible fight they could, just obviously didn't get the outcome we wanted. Yeah. I mean, it's good to kind of hear that immediate reflection of things that could have been done differently. And as you say, there's certainly a political factor, certainly in the UK, it's a vote winner to bash gambling. Unfortunately, that's just a reality that we kind of have to have to accept. But the argument against black markets, which I think we all in the industry understand, putting that forward, something I asked Stella David and Tain was, you know, we still see And I will point out that Andrew Rhodes, I've interviewed him, and he said that compliance has definitely improved over the years considerably.
mergedCandidates.title: We Emphasised Economics Over People
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: candid strategic critique—too much economic focus. Payoff: suggests shifting to consumer impact and highlights improved compliance narrative.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: What can we do differently, given that we've seen now how the government, the current government is going to act in the face of the methods we used before? Yeah, so I think there were some positives that came out of the budget, which we shouldn't forget. Obviously, machines gaming duty didn't increase. Obviously, there was a positive outcome on bingo duty. I think what could we have done differently? We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously, it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes. And if they were, where would they fall? You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit.
mergedCandidates.title: Balance Economics With Consumer Stories
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: reflective takeaway—campaign mis-focus exposed. Payoff: practical next step—add consumer impact evidence to strengthen future lobbying.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: We now have a really good process where we share learnings among the regulated operators and suppliers of things that they have learned through the process. And that obviously drives up standards even more in the illegal, harmful black market. You get none of that. They obviously pay no tax, but more worryingly, they offer no protection whatsoever. Customers don't know whether they're even going to get their money back. You know, they don't know where that money is going to, what it might be funding. So I don't think it undermines it. I think it probably underlines the importance of having robust regulations in the regulated sector so that customers know what they're getting and who they're who they're gaming with.
mergedCandidates.title: Regulated Learnings vs Black Market Void
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: striking contrast—regulated operators share learnings. Payoff: shows black market lacks oversight, consumer protection, and tax contribution.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: What can we do differently, given that we've seen now how the government, the current government is going to act in the face of the methods we used before? Yeah, so I think there were some positives that came out of the budget, which we shouldn't forget. Obviously, machines gaming duty didn't increase. Obviously, there was a positive outcome on bingo duty. I think what could we have done differently? We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously, it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes.
mergedCandidates.title: We Overplayed The Economic Case
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: contrarian admission—industry may have leaned too hard on economics. Payoff: offers a tactical pivot—add consumer voices next time for clearer impact.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: Will operators try and persuade more players into sports versus casino? I don't think that's doable because there is one P&L, right? So there's lots of benefits of people playing on both casino and sports. I think if you were to put in a kind of weird incentive, that just doesn't work for the player, doesn't work for the operator. So I don't see that happening.
mergedCandidates.title: You Can’t Shift Players To Sports
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: quick contrarian answer to a common speculation. Payoff: explains one-P&L reality and why incentives wouldn’t work—clear, snappy rebuttal.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: Obviously, machines gaming duty didn't increase. Obviously, there was a positive outcome on bingo duty. I think what could we have done differently? We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously, it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes. And if they were, where would they fall? You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit. But again, as I say, at the end of the day, the government took a political decision.
mergedCandidates.title: Budget Wins: Machines And Bingo Saved
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: unexpected positives inside a bad budget. Payoff: nuance—some duties unchanged, giving the industry tactical breathing room.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: We're currently going through the process of looking at what we might need in that role going forward, and then hopefully we'll be able to start recruiting soon. But I'm sure he'll continue being a friend and an advocate for us as an industry, as he always has been. Yeah. And I imagine you personally will probably be quite involved in that process because you'll be working very closely with the new chairman. Yeah, exactly. I think obviously with the tax increases, it's a chance to kind of pause and reflect what does the next kind of year, two, three years look like for the industry and therefore what do we need in that position to help us achieve that? I'm glad you mentioned the tax increases. You know, I've been here for over seven years and every year there's been some discussion, some threat of taxes might go up this year. And it didn't really happen, especially to this degree, until this day.
mergedCandidates.title: Recruiting A Chair During Tax Turmoil
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: leadership search framed by a crisis. Payoff: signals CEO involvement and the need for a chair who can navigate a taxed, shrinking market.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: You get none of that. They obviously pay no tax, but more worryingly, they offer no protection whatsoever. I think it probably underlines the importance of having robust regulations in the regulated sector so that customers know what they're getting and who they're who they're gaming with.
mergedCandidates.title: Black Market: No Tax, No Protection
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: blunt contrast—illegal sites pay no tax and offer no protections. Payoff: warns customers/operators about lost refunds and unknown funding, clear immediate stakes and behavioral consequence.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: I think obviously with the tax increases, it's a chance to kind of pause and reflect what does the next kind of year, two, three years look like for the industry and therefore what do we need in that position to help us achieve that? I'm glad you mentioned the tax increases. You know, I've been here for over seven years and every year there's been some discussion, some threat of taxes might go up this year. And it didn't really happen, especially to this degree, until this day.
mergedCandidates.title: Tax Hike Forces Industry Reckoning
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: personal reflection after unexpected tax shock. Payoff: signals an industry-wide rethink of leadership needs and strategy for the next 2–3 years.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: I think he's effectively the founding father, if you like, of the BGC because he set it up. He gave it the structure, the purpose, the integrity, you know, he drove the championing of industry standards and will therefore leave a really positive lasting legacy for the BGC. He's got a few big boots to fill. We're currently going through the process of looking at what we might need in that role going forward, and then hopefully we'll be able to start recruiting soon. But I'm sure he'll continue being a friend and an advocate for us as an industry, as he always has been. Yeah. And I imagine you personally will probably be quite involved in that process because you'll be working very closely with the new chairman.
mergedCandidates.title: What The New Chair Must Deliver
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: stakes—searching the right chair amid turmoil. Payoff: connects role requirements to tax-driven strategic priorities for the next years.

mergedCandidates.transcriptSnippet: To start with though, most recently Michael Duggar announced, there was an announcement that he's moving on as chairman. Could you maybe talk us through a bit of maybe what the process is going to be there, but also reflect on, of course, a big contribution from him to the BGC? Yeah, so I've been really lucky to have Michael as my chair and mentor for the last 18 months. Obviously as I transitioned into the CEO role, but also worked with him closely when I was a member of the BGC. I think he's effectively the founding father, if you like, of the BGC because he set it up.
mergedCandidates.title: BGC Founder Is Stepping Down
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: leadership change at the center of the industry. Payoff: outlines legacy, recruitment and why filling those 'big boots' matters organizationally.

mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: named figure increases credibility and curiosity. Payoff: recaps his structural impact and the recruitment challenge—informative but niche.

I think he's effectively the founding father, if you like, of the BGC because he set it up. mergedCandidates.title: Mentor Who Built The BGC
mergedCandidates.rationale: Hook: human angle—CEO’s mentor and founding architect. Payoff: summarizes legacy and the transition challenge, but lower broad appeal.

selectedClipTranscripts.title: UK Tax Hikes Fuel Black Market Boom
selectedClipTranscripts.rationale: Hook: bold claim—UK now among highest taxed markets. Payoff: immediate multi-party harm (customers, operators, Treasury, sports) with high urgency.
selectedClipTranscripts.transcript: You know, we've always been kind of seen as a proportionate, sensible place to do business from a tax and regulatory position. That is no longer the case now because of the political decision that the government made as the regulated sector has to adapt and unfortunately shrink as a result of the drastic tax increases, the black market is going to flourish. That's bad for customers, it's bad for operators, it's bad for the Treasury, it's bad for sports. I just simply don't understand the rationale behind the decision. The policies and their impact on kind of, as you say, the UK was how it's been seen in the past.

selectedClipTranscripts.title: Expect Reduced Odds And Fewer Venues
selectedClipTranscripts.rationale: Hook: consumer-facing, concrete harms—odds and places will shrink. Payoff: predicts less investment and innovation in UK, actionable and vivid.
selectedClipTranscripts.transcript: We're going to see offers and odds reduced. We're going to see, you know, less places. We're going to see reduced return to player. And I think more broadly, the businesses are going to have to look at diversifying outside of the UK because the UK, quite frankly, is now not an attractive market to be when you compare it to the rest of the world. So unfortunately, I think you will see less investment and innovation in the UK as a result of the budget increases. Will operators try and persuade more players into sports versus casino? I don't think that's doable because there is one P&L, right? So there's lots of benefits of people playing on both casino and sports.

selectedClipTranscripts.title: Fines Don’t Debunk Regulated Safety
selectedClipTranscripts.rationale: Hook: counters a common skeptic point—regulated fines exist. Payoff: explains why regulation still protects customers while black market offers none.
selectedClipTranscripts.transcript: Unfortunately, that's just a reality that we kind of have to have to accept. But the argument against black markets, which I think we all in the industry understand, putting that forward, something I asked Stella David and Tain was, you know, we still see And I will point out that Andrew Rhodes, I've interviewed him, and he said that compliance has definitely improved over the years considerably. But we still do see occasional fines. And we see kind of AML, or not so much AML, but maybe social kind of failings from leading regulated operators. Does that perhaps undermine the black market argument? Because you might just get a consumer maybe on Twitter saying, well, they've just been fined for the same thing that the industry saying black markets, black market operators are doing.

selectedClipTranscripts.title: Gambling Is A UK Vote-Winner
selectedClipTranscripts.rationale: Hook: provocative political insight—bashing gambling wins votes. Payoff: explains why policy choices ignore industry evidence and raise black market risk.
selectedClipTranscripts.transcript: We focused on the economic contribution that the industry provided, because obviously, it was a treasury decision on whether they were going to raise the taxes. And if they were, where would they fall? You know, potentially, we put too much effort and focus onto the economic arguments, and maybe we should have added some more of the kind of consumer impact and customer views on what the tax would have meant to balance it out a bit. But again, as I say, at the end of the day, the government took a political decision. And I do really feel like the industry put up the best possible fight they could, just obviously didn't get the outcome we wanted. Yeah. I mean, it's good to kind of hear that immediate reflection of things that could have been done differently. And as you say, there's certainly a political factor, certainly in the UK, it's a vote winner to bash gambling.

selectedClipTranscripts.title: Industry Didn’t Fail—Politics Decided
selectedClipTranscripts.rationale: Hook: rebuttal to claim of lobbying failure. Payoff: explains a data-led campaign still lost to political choice—clear cause and outcome.
selectedClipTranscripts.transcript: Unfortunately, the government took a political decision because of political reasons, which is outside of the industry's control. So I'd actually disagree that the industry failed in our efforts. Obviously, we were hugely disappointed to see the outcome. But I find it hard to see what more the industry collectively could have done to change the government's mind. So if you reflect on that, given what you've said about what you just said, that hard to see what you could have done more, but the outcome that we had in the end, and as you say, political and external factors, have you kind of had the chance to review strategically and think, well, what can we do next? What can we do differently, given that we've seen now how the government, the current government is going to act in the face of the methods we used before? Yeah, so I think there were some positives that came out of the budget, which we shouldn't forget.
